stages: Show students a short video or image of someone engaging in a healthy behaviour and ask them to name what they see and why it might matter.
Guide students to brainstorm all the health factors they know about and sort them into categories together.
Work with students to write simple explanations of how each factor supports health, using their own words.
Have students compare their explanations to the rubric levels and identify what makes an explanation complete versus incomplete.
Co-create anchor examples by selecting student work that clearly shows each level of understanding.
student_prompts: What health habits or choices do you see in this picture, and why do you think people do them?
What are all the different things that help keep our bodies and minds healthy?
How would you explain to a younger student why sleep is important for their health?
What would be missing from an explanation that just names factors but doesn't say how they help?
Can you think of a time when two health factors worked together to help you feel better?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Select student work that shows a clear progression from naming only one or two factors with vague reasons, to naming all four main factors with clear cause-and-effect statements, to showing how factors connect or apply to new situations. Anchor examples should use language and contexts familiar to students.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to explain how health factors support well-being.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some health factors like physical activity, nutrition, or sleep, but my explanations are unclear or not quite right.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify physical activity, nutrition, and sleep and explain how each one helps, but my explanations are short or don't show how they connect.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how physical activity, nutrition, sleep, and other factors support physical health and well-being outcomes.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can explain how multiple health factors work together and apply my reasoning to new health situations.

self_check_prompts: Did I name all the main health factors and explain how each one helps my body or mind?
Can I show how two or more factors work together, or use my understanding to explain a new health situation?

no_evidence: Ask the student to name one health habit they do every day and what they think it does for them.
Provide a simple sentence frame: 'When I [physical activity/nutrition/sleep], my body/mind feels [better/stronger/more alert] because …'
emerging: Ask the student to pick one factor they named and add one more detail about how it helps their health.
Prompt them to explain the same factor to a friend in their own words, then check if the explanation makes sense.
developing: Challenge the student to add one more factor beyond the three main ones and explain how it supports health.
Ask them to draw or describe a day where two factors worked together to help them feel good.
competent: Ask the student to predict what would happen to their health if one factor was missing for a week.
Invite them to explain how a health factor might affect someone in a different situation or with different needs.

stages: Show students video clips or live demonstrations of the same movement skill performed at different quality levels and ask them to describe what they notice about control and technique.
Guide students to identify the key differences between attempts that look shaky or incomplete versus smooth and controlled.
Work together to name what 'control,' 'coordination,' and 'technique' mean in the context of their own age-appropriate activities.
Have students sort movement examples into levels based on the language they've developed, then refine the descriptors together.
student_prompts: What do you see happening with the person's body when they perform this skill smoothly versus when they struggle?
What does it mean for a movement to have 'control' or 'good technique'?
How would you describe the difference between doing a skill with help and doing it all by yourself?
When might you need to change how you do a movement depending on where you are or what you're doing?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose movement examples from activities students actually do in class or at home, showing the same skill at clearly different levels of control and consistency. Select clips or demonstrations where the difference between shaky/incomplete and smooth/controlled is visually obvious.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to perform the movement or exercise skill.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can attempt movement skills with help, but my control and technique are not yet steady.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can perform basic movement and exercise skills on my own with mostly correct technique, though I am not always consistent.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can perform movement, exercise, and activity skills on my own with control, coordination, and technique that fit my age and what I am doing.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can perform movement skills with precision and change my technique smoothly when the activity or setting is different or more challenging.

self_check_prompts: Can I do this skill on my own, or do I still need help?
Is my movement smooth and controlled, or does it feel shaky or incomplete?

no_evidence: Invite the student to try the skill with you modeling it step by step alongside them.
Break the skill into smaller, simpler parts and practice one part at a time with encouragement.
emerging: Reduce the amount of support gradually and have the student practice the skill independently while you observe for moments of control.
Focus feedback on one specific part of the technique that, when improved, will make the whole movement smoother.
developing: Ask the student to perform the skill multiple times and notice which attempts feel most controlled; discuss what they did differently.
Introduce the skill in a slightly different context or with a small variation to build consistency across situations.
competent: Challenge the student to perform the skill in a new or more complex setting and reflect on how they adjusted their technique.
Encourage the student to teach or demonstrate the skill to a peer, which deepens their awareness of precision and control.

stages: Show students a short video or image of someone engaging in a self-care behaviour and ask them to describe what they notice and why it might matter.
Guide students to brainstorm a list of self-care behaviours they know about and sort them by category.
Work together to define what 'well-being' and 'sense of self-worth' mean in their own words.
Co-create descriptor language by asking students to explain how one self-care behaviour connects to well-being, then refine the explanation together.
Review the five levels as a class and have students identify which level matches sample student work.
student_prompts: What self-care behaviours do you see or know about, and what do you think happens when someone does them regularly?
How would you explain to a younger student why taking care of yourself matters for how you feel about yourself?
What does 'well-being' mean to you, and how is it different from just feeling happy for one day?
Can you think of a time when you took care of yourself and it made a difference—what changed?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose student work samples that show clear thinking about the link between a specific self-care behaviour and a concrete outcome for well-being; avoid samples that only list behaviours without explanation or that confuse self-care with other health topics.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to explain how self-care affects well-being.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some self-care behaviours, but my explanations are unclear or not quite accurate.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can explain on my own how some self-care behaviours help my well-being, but my reasoning is not complete.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain on my own how positive self-care behaviours support my well-being and how I feel about myself.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can explain self-care impact and connect it to how it shapes who I am and my relationships with others.

self_check_prompts: Can I name a self-care behaviour and explain how it helps my well-being, not just what it is?
Does my explanation show how self-care affects both how I feel and how I see myself?

no_evidence: Ask the student to pick one self-care behaviour they know and tell you what happens when someone does it.
Offer a sentence starter: 'When I [self-care behaviour], I feel … because …' and help them complete it.
emerging: Ask the student to pick one behaviour from their list and explain why it matters, not just what it is.
Prompt them to connect the behaviour to a feeling or outcome: 'How does this behaviour change how you feel or think about yourself?'
developing: Ask the student to explain a second self-care behaviour and how it supports well-being, to build completeness.
Encourage them to think about both immediate and longer-term effects: 'What happens right away, and what happens over time?'
competent: Invite them to explore how one self-care behaviour affects their relationships or how others see them.
Ask them to consider how self-care connects to their identity: 'How does taking care of yourself shape who you are?'

stages: Show students a short video or image of someone experiencing an emotion and ask them to name what they see.
Guide students to brainstorm their own experiences and the feelings that came with them.
Work together to identify what makes a connection between experience and feeling clear and strong.
Co-create language for each level that describes how well someone can explain this connection and its effect on well-being.
student_prompts: What experience have you had that changed how you felt?
How did that feeling affect you—your body, your thoughts, or how you treated others?
What words would help someone understand the link between what happened and how it made you feel?
When you explain this connection really well, what details do you include that make it clear?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose one anchor that shows a clear, simple connection between an experience and a feeling, and one that goes deeper by explaining how that feeling shaped the student's well-being or behavior over time. Look for examples where the student's own voice is present and the reasoning is visible.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to connect an experience to a feeling or explained how it affected my well-being.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name one experience and one feeling, but I need help explaining how they are connected.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can describe an experience and the feeling it caused, but I do not fully explain how it affected my mental health or well-being.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can explain how a specific experience and my emotional response influenced my mental health and well-being.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can explain how experiences and emotional responses influence my well-being and connect this to patterns I notice in my life.

self_check_prompts: Can I describe a real experience and name the feeling it caused?
Can I explain how that feeling affected my well-being, my thoughts, or my actions?

no_evidence: Ask the student to share one thing that happened to them recently and how it made them feel.
Model aloud how you connect an experience to a feeling, then ask them to try with their own example.
emerging: Ask follow-up questions to deepen the connection: How did that feeling change what you did next or how you felt about yourself?
Provide a sentence frame to help them explain the link: When [experience] happened, I felt [feeling] because …
developing: Ask the student to explain one way their feeling affected their well-being, such as their sleep, mood, or relationships.
Encourage them to add one more detail that shows why this experience mattered to their mental health.
competent: Invite the student to explore a second experience and compare how different experiences shape their well-being differently.
Ask them to reflect on whether they notice patterns in how certain types of experiences affect them over time.

stages: Show students a real decision scenario and ask them to list all the people and groups affected by it.
Guide students to distinguish between immediate impacts and broader societal ripples.
Introduce the concept of influencing factors and have students brainstorm what shapes how people decide.
Work together to sort impacts and factors into categories and discuss what makes reasoning complete or incomplete.
Co-author level descriptors by asking students what evidence would show they can do each level.
student_prompts: Who is affected by this decision, and in what ways?
What happens beyond the immediate situation—how might this decision affect the wider community or society?
What things influence how someone makes a decision—their beliefs, circumstances, information, or something else?
How do you know if your analysis of a decision is thorough or if you've missed something important?
Can you think of a time when one decision's impact led to another decision being made?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose decisions that are age-appropriate and familiar to students, with clear impacts on multiple levels (personal, peer, community). Select examples where influencing factors are visible but not obvious, so students must reason through them.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to analyse how decisions affect people or what influences decision-making.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some people or groups affected by a decision and name some factors that influence decisions, but my reasoning has gaps or mistakes.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can independently identify how a decision affects myself and others, and I can name some factors that influence decisions, but I do not yet consider the wider society.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can independently analyse how a decision impacts myself, others, and society, and I can identify the key factors that influence how people decide.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyse decision impacts and influencing factors across different situations and recognise how influences are connected to each other.

self_check_prompts: Have I considered how this decision affects me, the people around me, and the wider community?
Can I explain what factors influenced this decision and how they are connected?

no_evidence: Ask the student to name one person or group affected by a decision and describe what happened to them.
Prompt the student to think of one thing that might make someone choose one way instead of another.
emerging: Help the student check their reasoning by asking 'How do you know that?' or 'What evidence shows that?'
Guide the student to add one more impact or factor they may have overlooked.
developing: Ask the student to step back and consider: 'Who else might be affected by this decision beyond the people we've already named?'
Encourage the student to explore how the decision might affect the community or society as a whole.
competent: Invite the student to explore a different context or scenario and apply their analysis skills there.
Ask the student to trace how one influencing factor connects to or affects another factor.

stages: Show students a real or realistic decision scenario and ask them to list every possible outcome they can think of.
Guide students to sort their outcomes into categories: effects on self, effects on others, short-term, and long-term.
Introduce the idea of risk and have students identify which outcomes are risky or uncertain.
Work together to distinguish between surface-level consequences and deeper, connected consequences that ripple outward.
Co-author level descriptors by asking students what it looks like when someone does or does not think through implications before deciding.
student_prompts: What could happen if you make this decision—for you and for people around you?
Which of these consequences might happen soon, and which might take weeks or months to show up?
What could go wrong, and how confident are you that you've thought of all the risks?
If one consequence happens, what else might that trigger down the line?
How would you describe someone who rushes into a decision without thinking, and someone who carefully weighs what might happen?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose decisions that are age-appropriate and personally relevant to students, with consequences that span both immediate and delayed effects, and that touch multiple people or contexts. Look for examples where surface-level thinking misses important ripple effects.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to think about what might happen before I decide.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some things that might happen if I make a decision, but I need help and my thinking might be incomplete or mixed up.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can figure out on my own what might happen for me and for others, but I might miss consequences that take longer to show up or affect people further away.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can think through several different implications and risks of a decision for myself and others before I decide.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can evaluate how implications and risks connect across different situations and predict consequences that trigger other consequences.

self_check_prompts: Have I thought about what happens to me and to other people if I make this choice?
Am I seeing only what happens right away, or am I also thinking about what might happen later or in other areas of my life?

no_evidence: Ask the student to pause and name one thing that could happen if they make this decision.
Model thinking aloud about a consequence, then ask them to name one more consequence you did not mention.
emerging: Ask: Who else might be affected by this decision, and what might happen to them?
Help the student separate immediate consequences from ones that might take longer to appear.
developing: Push the student to think beyond the people directly involved: What other situations or people might be touched by this decision?
Ask: If that consequence happens, what else might it cause to happen?
competent: Invite the student to map out how one consequence might trigger a chain of other consequences.
Challenge them to identify a risk they had not considered by asking about less obvious contexts or longer time horizons.

stages: Show students a career choice scenario and ask them to brainstorm all the ways that choice might affect someone's life over time.
Guide students to sort their brainstormed consequences into three domains: personal, financial, and social.
Work together to distinguish between surface-level and significant consequences, discussing what makes a consequence worth describing.
Co-create descriptors for each level by asking students what evidence would show they can identify and describe consequences well.
student_prompts: If someone chose to become a teacher, what are all the ways that decision might change their life—not just at the start, but years later?
Which of these consequences fit into personal (how you feel, your skills, your lifestyle), financial (money and resources), or social (relationships, community, status) categories?
Which consequences are surface-level and which ones are truly significant or life-changing?
What would it look like if you could identify consequences in all three domains with real detail and accuracy?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose career scenarios that naturally generate consequences across all three domains and that students can relate to or research easily. Select student work samples that show varying levels of depth—from naming one consequence vaguely to explaining how a financial consequence ripples into personal and social effects over decades.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to identify or describe consequences of career choices.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name one or two consequences of a career choice, but my descriptions are unclear or not quite accurate.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify consequences across one or two domains on my own, but I miss some important details or do not explore them deeply enough.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can identify and describe significant lifelong consequences of career choices across personal, financial, and social domains.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can identify consequences and explain how they connect to each other across domains or how they change over time.

self_check_prompts: Have I identified consequences in all three domains: personal, financial, and social?
Are my descriptions detailed and accurate, and do they show why these consequences matter over a lifetime?

no_evidence: Ask the student to pick one career and name just one thing that might change in their life because of that choice.
Provide a sentence starter: 'If someone chose this career, they might experience a change in their [personal life / money / relationships] because…'
emerging: Ask the student to pick one consequence they named and add two or three details that explain why it matters.
Introduce the three domains and ask: 'Which domain does this consequence belong to, and can you find one consequence in a different domain?'
developing: Ask the student to identify at least one consequence in the domain they have not yet explored.
Prompt them to explain how one consequence might lead to another consequence over time.
competent: Ask the student to trace how a consequence in one domain affects or connects to consequences in another domain.
Invite them to predict how a consequence might change or deepen as someone moves through different life stages.

stages: Show students a short video or image of a cultural practice and ask them to list what they notice without judgment.
Guide students to distinguish between a norm they observed and the reasons or values that might underlie it.
Introduce the idea of comparing how the same value appears differently across two cultures.
Work together to identify what 'depth of analysis' means by examining a weak versus strong explanation of cultural development.
Co-create the five levels by having students sort example statements into categories of quality.
student_prompts: What norm or value did you spot in this cultural practice, and what clues tell you it matters to that community?
Why might this norm have developed in this culture—what problem or need might it solve?
How is this value expressed differently in another culture you know about, and what might explain the difference?
What makes an explanation of cultural norms 'surface-level' versus 'deep'—what details matter?
Which of these statements about cultural norms shows the strongest thinking, and why?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose anchor examples that show a clear progression from naming a norm without context, to explaining its origin in one culture, to comparing how similar values function differently across two or more cultures. Prioritize examples that reveal student reasoning, not just factual knowledge.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet attempted to analyse how cultural norms or values develop.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can identify some norms or values in a culture with support, but my explanation of why they developed is unclear or missing cultural context.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can independently explain how norms develop within one culture, but I struggle to compare how similar norms function across different cultures.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can analyse how norms and values develop within and across different cultural contexts and explain my reasoning clearly.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyse how norms and values develop across cultures and connect these patterns to larger social systems and historical forces.

self_check_prompts: Can I explain not just what a norm is, but why it developed and what it does for that culture?
Have I compared how the same value or need shows up differently in at least two cultures, and explained what causes those differences?

no_evidence: Ask the student to name one norm they observe in a cultural practice and describe what it looks like in action.
Prompt them to think of a reason that norm might exist—what does it help the community do or believe?
emerging: Help the student dig deeper by asking 'What in the environment, history, or beliefs of this culture might have caused this norm to develop?'
Introduce a second culture and ask how a similar value or need is expressed there, then guide them to notice the difference.
developing: Challenge the student to select a second culture and systematically compare how the same underlying value appears in different forms.
Ask them to explain what factors in each culture might account for the different expressions of that value.
competent: Invite the student to explore how the norms they analysed connect to larger patterns in society, such as economics, power, or survival.
Encourage them to consider how understanding these patterns helps explain cultural change over time.

stages: Show students a short scenario of a relationship and ask them to spot what feels wrong or concerning.
Guide students to name the specific behaviours or patterns they noticed and discuss why those matter.
Work together to sort their observations into categories of warning signs.
Build shared language for what 'analysis' means by asking students how they would explain why a warning sign matters.
Co-author level descriptors by asking students what it looks like when someone can do this well, with help, or not yet.
student_prompts: What behaviours or words in this relationship made you uncomfortable or worried?
Why do you think that behaviour is a warning sign rather than just a normal disagreement?
What pattern do you see—does this warning sign connect to other problems in the relationship?
How would you explain to someone else why this relationship dynamic is unhealthy?
co_construction_plan.anchor_examples_guidance: Choose scenarios that show clear warning signs but vary in context and subtlety—one obvious example and one more nuanced case help students see that analysis requires looking beyond surface behaviour. Avoid examples that centre on only one type of relationship or harm.

levels.name: no_evidence
levels.descriptor: I have not yet tried to identify warning signs or explain what makes a relationship unhealthy.

levels.name: emerging
levels.descriptor: I can name some warning signs when someone helps me, but my explanation of why they matter is incomplete or not quite accurate.

levels.name: developing
levels.descriptor: I can identify several warning signs on my own, but my analysis does not go deep enough or does not work well when the situation is new or different.

levels.name: competent
levels.descriptor: I can analyse relationship dynamics on my own and accurately identify multiple warning signs that show a relationship is unhealthy.

levels.name: extending
levels.descriptor: I can analyse relationship dynamics in many different contexts and connect warning signs to the deeper patterns of harm underneath them.

self_check_prompts: Can I name at least three warning signs and explain why each one matters?
Have I looked at the relationship as a whole pattern, or just picked out single moments?

no_evidence: Ask the student to point to one behaviour in the scenario and describe what they see happening.
Offer a sentence starter: 'This is a warning sign because…' and ask them to complete it.
emerging: Ask the student to explain why each warning sign they named is unhealthy rather than just normal conflict.
Prompt them to find a second or third warning sign they might have missed in the same scenario.
developing: Ask the student to show how two or three warning signs connect to each other or repeat over time.
Introduce a slightly different context and ask them to identify whether the same warning signs apply and why.
competent: Ask the student to explain what deeper need or power imbalance might be driving the warning signs they identified.
Challenge them to analyse a relationship scenario from a perspective they have not considered before.
